text.title: User Story Mapping: Workshop and Agent Workflow
text.summary: Workshop facilitation agenda, participant roles, common mistakes, and agent-specific gotchas for BA-led story mapping.

rule.statement: Require all five participant roles in the workshop: Product Owner, Business Analyst, Developer, UX Designer, and at least one key stakeholder; refuse to proceed if the decision-maker is absent.
rule.rationale: A half-attended map produces false consensus — absent stakeholders discover the release plan only at review, triggering scope changes that invalidate the map's value.

list.item: Duration: 2-4 hours for a complete map; break into two sessions if scope exceeds 5 activities.
Materials: large wall or whiteboard, sticky notes in 3 colors (activities/tasks/stories), markers.
Agenda: personas + goals (15 min) → backbone activities (30 min) → tasks (30 min) → stories (60 min) → vertical prioritization (30 min) → release slices (30 min).

antipattern.description: Flat backlog — stories written without journey context.
antipattern.reason: No horizontal axis means no way to detect missing tasks or trace requirements to process steps.

antipattern.description: Technical stories on the map ("As a system, I want to batch-process orders").
antipattern.reason: INVEST requires user perspective; technical stories belong in a separate architectural backlog, not the story map.

antipattern.description: No release lines defined — map becomes a prioritized list.
antipattern.reason: Without horizontal slice boundaries, the team has no commitment to what ships together and cannot plan iterations.
Workshops optimize the happy path; add a red-sticky row for exception paths below each task band so they are visible, not forgotten.

rule.statement: Run story mapping as a four-stage subagent pipeline: elicitation prep → workshop facilitation → artifact translation → validation loop.
rule.rationale: Each stage requires different model capabilities (research, structured elicitation, formatting, critique); a single prompt produces weaker output than staged handoffs.

rule.statement: Do not let the agent decide release boundaries; slicing is a business and risk decision. Lock the agent to "suggest releases" mode, never "commit releases".
rule.rationale: Release boundaries require authority the agent does not have; auto-committed slices bypass stakeholder sign-off and invalidate the validation loop.

rule.statement: After story generation, run an INVEST critic pass: reject stories that are too large (epic in disguise) or non-independent ("as a system").
rule.rationale: LLMs produce stories that pass a superficial format check but violate INVEST; an explicit critic pass before BA acceptance catches these failures cheaply.

example.note: Facilitation prompt pattern constrains the agent to grounded output only.
example.code: You are a BABOK-aligned BA facilitator. Persona pack: {personas}. Current backbone: {activities}. We are decomposing activity "{a}" into tasks. Ask 3 elicitation questions targeting the persona's goal, constraints, and exception path. Output JSON: {questions: [], suggested_tasks: []}. No prose. No invented tasks not grounded in a question.

example.note: Translation prompt enforces full BA artifact output with gap detection.
example.code: Input: story-map.json (activities, tasks, stories, releases). For each Release-1 story emit: BRD section (problem, scope, AC), BPMN participant + task, RTM row (story_id, requirement_id, process_step, test_case_id). Flag any story missing AC or persona reference. Output strict JSON matching schema {brd, bpmn, rtm, gaps}.

rule.statement: Enforce four mandatory human checkpoints: backbone approval before tasks, Release-1 slice approval before stories, BRD+RTM diff review before publication, NFR register review before sign-off.
rule.rationale: Each checkpoint prevents downstream artifact cascades from building on a flawed foundation; skipping any one checkpoint increases rework cost by at least one stage.
